title: Epsilon Characterization of Suprema
learning_objective: Use the definition of least upper bound to prove the epsilon characterization of a supremum and its infimum analogue.
preliminaries: Let \(S\) be a nonempty subset of \(\mathbb{R}\). A number \(b\) is \(\sup S\) if \(s \le b\) for every \(s \in S\), and if \(c<b\), then \(c\) is not an upper bound for \(S\). A number \(a\) is \(\inf S\) if \(a \le s\) for every \(s \in S\), and if \(c>a\), then \(c\) is not a lower bound for \(S\).
question: Prove that if \(b=\sup S\), then for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(s_\varepsilon \in S\) such that \(s_\varepsilon>b-\varepsilon\). State and prove the analogous epsilon characterization of \(a=\inf S\).
solution:
Fix \(\varepsilon>0\). Since \(b=\sup S\), the number \(b-\varepsilon\) is strictly smaller than \(b\), so it cannot be an upper bound for \(S\). Therefore some \(s_\varepsilon \in S\) satisfies \(s_\varepsilon>b-\varepsilon\). This proves the supremum statement. Notice that the upper-bound part of the definition also gives \(s_\varepsilon \le b\), so the point lies in the interval \((b-\varepsilon,b]\).

For the infimum statement, if \(a=\inf S\), then for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(s_\varepsilon \in S\) such that \(s_\varepsilon<a+\varepsilon\). To prove this, fix \(\varepsilon>0\). The number \(a+\varepsilon\) is strictly larger than \(a\), so it cannot be a lower bound for \(S\). Hence there is \(s_\varepsilon \in S\) with \(s_\varepsilon<a+\varepsilon\). The lower-bound part of the definition also gives \(a \le s_\varepsilon\).

source.notes: Rewrites the local exercise into a standalone proof question.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Supremum Need Not Be Attained
learning_objective: Distinguish least upper bounds from maximum elements using a minimal counterexample.
preliminaries: A maximum of \(S \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) is an element \(m \in S\) such that \(s \le m\) for every \(s \in S\). A supremum of \(S\) is the least upper bound of \(S\), and need not belong to \(S\).
question: Decide whether the following statement is true or false: if a nonempty set \(S \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) has a supremum, then \(\sup S \in S\). Give a proof or a counterexample. Then state an additional condition under which the statement becomes true.
solution:
The statement is false. Let \(S=(0,1)\). The number \(1\) is an upper bound for \(S\), and every \(c<1\) fails to be an upper bound because one can choose \(s\in(0,1)\) with \(c<s<1\). Thus \(\sup S=1\), but \(1\notin S\).

One additional condition that makes the statement true is compactness, for instance if \(S\) is nonempty and compact in \(\mathbb{R}\). The function \(f(s)=s\) is continuous, so by the extreme value theorem it attains its maximum on \(S\). If \(m=\max S\), then \(m\) is an upper bound and belongs to \(S\); since no smaller number can be an upper bound, \(m=\sup S\).

source.notes: Converts a true-false item into a standalone counterexample and repair question.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: A Noncompact Interval via Open Covers
learning_objective: Use the open-cover definition to show why a nonclosed bounded set need not be compact.
preliminaries: A collection \(\mathcal{U}\) of open subsets of \(\mathbb{R}\) is an open cover of \(S\subseteq\mathbb{R}\) if \(S\subseteq \bigcup_{U\in\mathcal{U}} U\). The set \(S\) is compact if every open cover of \(S\) has a finite subcover.
question: Construct an open cover of \(S=(0,1]\) that has no finite subcover. Use it to prove that \(S\) is not compact.
solution:
For each integer \(n\ge 2\), let \(U_n=(1/n,2)\). The collection \(\mathcal{U}=\{U_n:n\ge 2\}\) covers \((0,1]\): if \(x\in(0,1]\), choose \(n>1/x\), so \(1/n<x\), and hence \(x\in U_n\).

Now take any finite subcollection \(U_{n_1},\ldots,U_{n_k}\). Let \(N=\max\{n_1,\ldots,n_k\}\). Since \(1/N \ge 1/n_j\) for every selected \(n_j\), the union of the selected sets is \((1/N,2)\). The point \(x=1/(2N)\) belongs to \((0,1]\) but not to \((1/N,2)\). Thus no finite subcollection covers \(S\). Therefore \(S\) is not compact.

source.notes: Standalone version of the open-cover compactness exercise.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Quasiconcavity and Upper Contour Sets
learning_objective: Prove the equivalence between quasiconcavity and convex upper contour sets.
preliminaries:
Let \(X\subseteq\mathbb{R}^d\) be convex. A function \(f:X\to\mathbb{R}\) is quasiconcave if for every \(x,y\in X\) and every \(\alpha\in[0,1]\),
\[
f(\alpha x+(1-\alpha)y)\ge \min\{f(x),f(y)\}.
\]
For \(r\in\mathbb{R}\), the upper contour set at level \(r\) is \(U_r=\{x\in X:f(x)\ge r\}\).
question: Prove that \(f\) is quasiconcave if and only if \(U_r\) is convex for every \(r\in\mathbb{R}\).
solution:
First suppose \(f\) is quasiconcave. Fix \(r\in\mathbb{R}\) and take \(x,y\in U_r\). Then \(f(x)\ge r\) and \(f(y)\ge r\). For any \(\alpha\in[0,1]\), quasiconcavity gives
\[
f(\alpha x+(1-\alpha)y)\ge \min\{f(x),f(y)\}\ge r.
\]
Thus \(\alpha x+(1-\alpha)y\in U_r\), so \(U_r\) is convex.

Conversely, suppose every \(U_r\) is convex. Fix \(x,y\in X\) and \(\alpha\in[0,1]\). Let \(r=\min\{f(x),f(y)\}\). Then \(x,y\in U_r\). By convexity of \(U_r\), \(\alpha x+(1-\alpha)y\in U_r\), which means
\[
f(\alpha x+(1-\alpha)y)\ge r=\min\{f(x),f(y)\}.
\]
Therefore \(f\) is quasiconcave.

source.notes: Expands a proposition-proof exercise with full definitions.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Upper Hemicontinuity from a Closed Graph
learning_objective: Prove a standard closed-graph sufficient condition for upper hemicontinuity.
preliminaries: Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be metric spaces and let \(F:X\rightrightarrows Y\) be a correspondence. Its graph is \(\operatorname{Gr}(F)=\{(x,y):y\in F(x)\}\). The correspondence is upper hemicontinuous at \(x\) if for every open set \(V\subseteq Y\) with \(F(x)\subseteq V\), there is a neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\) such that \(F(x')\subseteq V\) for all \(x'\in U\).
question: Assume \(Y\) is compact, \(F(x)\) is nonempty for every \(x\), and \(\operatorname{Gr}(F)\) is closed in \(X\times Y\). Prove that \(F\) is upper hemicontinuous.
solution:
Fix \(x\in X\) and an open set \(V\subseteq Y\) such that \(F(x)\subseteq V\). Suppose upper hemicontinuity fails. Then for every neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\), there is \(x'\in U\) and \(y'\in F(x')\setminus V\). In metric terms, choose a sequence \(x_n\to x\) and \(y_n\in F(x_n)\setminus V\).

Because \(Y\) is compact, the sequence \((y_n)\) has a convergent subsequence \(y_{n_k}\to y\in Y\). Since \(V\) is open and \(y_{n_k}\notin V\), the limit \(y\) lies in the closed set \(Y\setminus V\). Also \(x_{n_k}\to x\), and \((x_{n_k},y_{n_k})\in\operatorname{Gr}(F)\). Closedness of the graph implies \((x,y)\in\operatorname{Gr}(F)\), so \(y\in F(x)\). But \(F(x)\subseteq V\), while \(y\notin V\), a contradiction. Thus \(F\) is upper hemicontinuous at \(x\), and since \(x\) was arbitrary, it is upper hemicontinuous.

source.notes: Self-contained theorem variant aligned with correspondence exercises.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: KKT Conditions as Sufficient Conditions
learning_objective: Show why KKT first-order conditions are sufficient under concavity and convex constraints.
preliminaries:
Let \(X=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^d:g_j(x)\ge 0,\ j=1,\ldots,m\}\). Suppose \(f\) and each \(g_j\) are differentiable, \(f\) is concave, and each \(g_j\) is concave, so \(X\) is convex. A KKT pair \((x^*,\lambda^*)\) satisfies feasibility, \(\lambda_j^*\ge 0\), complementary slackness \(\lambda_j^*g_j(x^*)=0\), and
\[
\nabla f(x^*)+\sum_{j=1}^m \lambda_j^*\nabla g_j(x^*)=0.
\]
question: Prove that if \(x^*\in X\) has multipliers \(\lambda^*\) satisfying the KKT conditions above, then \(x^*\) maximizes \(f\) over \(X\).
solution:
Take any \(x\in X\). By concavity of \(f\),
\[
f(x)-f(x^*)\le \nabla f(x^*)\cdot (x-x^*).
\]
Using stationarity,
\[
\nabla f(x^*)\cdot (x-x^*)=-\sum_{j=1}^m \lambda_j^*\nabla g_j(x^*)\cdot (x-x^*).
\]
By concavity of each \(g_j\),
\[
g_j(x)-g_j(x^*)\le \nabla g_j(x^*)\cdot (x-x^*),
\]
so \(-\nabla g_j(x^*)\cdot (x-x^*)\le g_j(x^*)-g_j(x)\). Therefore
\[
f(x)-f(x^*)\le \sum_{j=1}^m \lambda_j^*(g_j(x^*)-g_j(x)).
\]
Complementary slackness gives \(\lambda_j^*g_j(x^*)=0\), while feasibility gives \(g_j(x)\ge 0\) and \(\lambda_j^*\ge 0\). Hence the right side is
\[
-\sum_{j=1}^m \lambda_j^* g_j(x)\le 0.
\]
Thus \(f(x)\le f(x^*)\) for every feasible \(x\), so \(x^*\) is a global maximizer.

source.notes: Standalone proof of a standard optimization result used in the notes.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Envelope Theorem for an Interior Maximum
learning_objective: Derive the envelope formula and understand why derivatives of the optimizer drop out.
preliminaries: Let \(f:\mathbb{R}\times\Theta\to\mathbb{R}\) be continuously differentiable. For each parameter \(\theta\), suppose \(x(\theta)\) is an interior maximizer of \(f(\cdot,\theta)\), and define \(V(\theta)=f(x(\theta),\theta)\). Assume \(x(\theta)\) is differentiable and the first-order condition \(f_x(x(\theta),\theta)=0\) holds.
question:
Prove that \(V'(\theta)=f_\theta(x(\theta),\theta)\). Then apply the result to
\[
f(x,\theta)=\theta\log x-x
\]
on \(x>0\) for \(\theta>0\).
solution:
By the chain rule,
\[
V'(\theta)=f_x(x(\theta),\theta)x'(\theta)+f_\theta(x(\theta),\theta).
\]
The first-order condition at the interior optimum says \(f_x(x(\theta),\theta)=0\). Therefore the first term is zero and
\[
V'(\theta)=f_\theta(x(\theta),\theta).
\]

For \(f(x,\theta)=\theta\log x-x\), the first-order condition is \(\theta/x-1=0\), so \(x(\theta)=\theta\). The value function is \(V(\theta)=\theta\log\theta-\theta\). Direct differentiation gives \(V'(\theta)=\log\theta\). The envelope formula gives \(f_\theta(x,\theta)=\log x\), hence \(f_\theta(x(\theta),\theta)=\log\theta\), the same answer.

source.notes: Clean parametric optimization version of the comparative-statics material.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Cobb-Douglas Marshallian Demand
learning_objective: Derive Marshallian demand, indirect utility, and expenditure shares for Cobb-Douglas preferences.
preliminaries: A Marshallian demand solves \(\max_{x\ge 0} u(x)\) subject to \(p\cdot x\le w\), where \(p\gg 0\) and \(w>0\).
question: Let \(u(x_1,x_2)=x_1^\alpha x_2^{1-\alpha}\), where \(\alpha\in(0,1)\). For prices \(p_1,p_2>0\) and wealth \(w>0\), derive the Marshallian demand and indirect utility. Show that each good receives a constant expenditure share.
solution:
Since utility is strictly increasing, the budget binds: \(p_1x_1+p_2x_2=w\). For an interior optimum, the Lagrangian is
\[
\mathcal{L}=x_1^\alpha x_2^{1-\alpha}+\lambda(w-p_1x_1-p_2x_2).
\]
The first-order conditions imply
\[
\alpha x_1^{\alpha-1}x_2^{1-\alpha}=\lambda p_1,\qquad
(1-\alpha)x_1^\alpha x_2^{-\alpha}=\lambda p_2.
\]
Dividing gives
\[
\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}\frac{x_2}{x_1}=\frac{p_1}{p_2}.
\]
Thus \(p_1x_1=\alpha w\) and \(p_2x_2=(1-\alpha)w\). The demand is
\[
x_1(p,w)=\frac{\alpha w}{p_1},\qquad x_2(p,w)=\frac{(1-\alpha)w}{p_2}.
\]
Substitution yields indirect utility
\[
v(p,w)=\left(\frac{\alpha w}{p_1}\right)^\alpha
\left(\frac{(1-\alpha)w}{p_2}\right)^{1-\alpha}.
\]
The expenditure shares are \(p_1x_1/w=\alpha\) and \(p_2x_2/w=1-\alpha\), independent of prices and wealth.

source.notes: Canonical self-contained version of a standard Varian-style demand exercise.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Cobb-Douglas Demand with Three Goods
learning_objective: Generalize Cobb-Douglas expenditure-share logic to three goods.
preliminaries: A consumer has prices \(p\gg 0\), wealth \(w>0\), and chooses \(x\ge 0\) to maximize utility subject to \(p\cdot x\le w\).
question: Let \(u(x)=x_1^{1/2}x_2^{1/3}x_3^{1/6}\). Derive the Marshallian demand for all three goods and compute indirect utility.
solution:
The exponents sum to one and all are positive, so the optimum is interior and the budget binds. For Cobb-Douglas utility \(\prod_i x_i^{a_i}\) with \(\sum_i a_i=1\), the first-order conditions imply \(p_ix_i=a_iw\). Here \(a=(1/2,1/3,1/6)\). Therefore
\[
x_1=\frac{w}{2p_1},\qquad x_2=\frac{w}{3p_2},\qquad x_3=\frac{w}{6p_3}.
\]
Substituting into utility gives
\[
v(p,w)=\left(\frac{w}{2p_1}\right)^{1/2}
\left(\frac{w}{3p_2}\right)^{1/3}
\left(\frac{w}{6p_3}\right)^{1/6}.
\]
The expenditure shares are \(1/2\), \(1/3\), and \(1/6\).

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Expenditure Function and Shephard's Lemma
learning_objective: Compute Hicksian demands from an expenditure function and verify Shephard's lemma.
preliminaries: The expenditure function is \(e(p,u)=\min_{x\ge 0}\{p\cdot x:U(x)\ge u\}\). If \(e\) is differentiable in prices, Shephard's lemma says \(h_i(p,u)=\partial e(p,u)/\partial p_i\), where \(h\) is Hicksian demand.
question: For \(U(x_1,x_2)=x_1^\alpha x_2^{1-\alpha}\) with \(\alpha\in(0,1)\), derive the expenditure function and Hicksian demand.
solution:
The expenditure minimization problem is
\[
\min_{x_1,x_2\ge 0} p_1x_1+p_2x_2 \quad\text{s.t.}\quad x_1^\alpha x_2^{1-\alpha}\ge u.
\]
At the optimum the utility constraint binds and the tangency condition gives
\[
\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}\frac{x_2}{x_1}=\frac{p_1}{p_2}.
\]
Solving this with \(x_1^\alpha x_2^{1-\alpha}=u\) yields
\[
h_1(p,u)=u\left(\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{1-\alpha},\qquad
h_2(p,u)=u\left(\frac{1-\alpha}{\alpha}\frac{p_1}{p_2}\right)^\alpha.
\]
Expenditure is \(e(p,u)=p_1h_1+p_2h_2\), which simplifies to
\[
e(p,u)=u\frac{p_1^\alpha p_2^{1-\alpha}}{\alpha^\alpha(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha}}.
\]
Differentiating gives
\[
\frac{\partial e}{\partial p_1}
=u\frac{\alpha p_1^{\alpha-1}p_2^{1-\alpha}}{\alpha^\alpha(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha}}
=u\left(\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{1-\alpha}=h_1(p,u),
\]
and similarly \(\partial e/\partial p_2=h_2(p,u)\).

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Checking WARP with Two Observations
learning_objective: Apply revealed preference inequalities to determine whether finite demand data violate WARP.
preliminaries: Observation \(t\) consists of prices \(p^t\gg 0\), wealth \(w^t\), and chosen bundle \(x^t\) with \(p^t\cdot x^t=w^t\). Bundle \(x^t\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^s\) if \(p^t\cdot x^t\ge p^t\cdot x^s\). WARP requires that if \(x^t\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^s\) and \(x^t\ne x^s\), then \(x^s\) is not directly revealed preferred to \(x^t\).
question:
A consumer is observed twice:
\[
p^1=(1,2),\quad x^1=(2,1),
\]
and
\[
p^2=(2,1),\quad x^2=(1,2).
\]
Does this data set satisfy WARP?
solution:
At observation 1, expenditure on the chosen bundle is
\[
p^1\cdot x^1=1\cdot 2+2\cdot 1=4.
\]
The cost of \(x^2\) at prices \(p^1\) is
\[
p^1\cdot x^2=1\cdot 1+2\cdot 2=5.
\]
Thus \(x^2\) was not affordable at observation 1, so \(x^1\) is not directly revealed preferred to \(x^2\).

At observation 2,
\[
p^2\cdot x^2=2\cdot 1+1\cdot 2=4,
\]
while
\[
p^2\cdot x^1=2\cdot 2+1\cdot 1=5.
\]
Thus \(x^1\) was not affordable at observation 2, so \(x^2\) is not directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\). Since there is no revealed preference cycle, WARP is satisfied.

source.notes: Standalone finite-data WARP drill inspired by revealed preference material.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: A Two-Observation WARP Violation
learning_objective: Construct and diagnose a minimal violation of WARP.
preliminaries: Observation \(t\) consists of prices \(p^t\gg 0\), wealth \(w^t\), and chosen bundle \(x^t\). Bundle \(x^t\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^s\) if \(p^t\cdot x^t\ge p^t\cdot x^s\). WARP rules out mutual direct revealed preference between two distinct chosen bundles.
question:
A consumer is observed twice:
\[
p^1=(1,1),\quad x^1=(2,0),
\]
and
\[
p^2=(1,1),\quad x^2=(0,2).
\]
Does this data set satisfy WARP? Explain.
solution:
At observation 1, \(p^1\cdot x^1=2\) and \(p^1\cdot x^2=2\). Thus \(x^2\) was affordable when \(x^1\) was chosen, so \(x^1\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^2\).

At observation 2, \(p^2\cdot x^2=2\) and \(p^2\cdot x^1=2\). Thus \(x^1\) was affordable when \(x^2\) was chosen, so \(x^2\) is directly revealed preferred to \(x^1\).

Because \(x^1\ne x^2\), mutual direct revealed preference violates WARP. The data cannot be generated by a consumer with a stable preference that always chooses a unique best affordable bundle.

source.notes: Counterexample variant for finite revealed preference data.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Full Insurance under Actuarially Fair Pricing
learning_objective: Show that a strictly risk-averse expected-utility maximizer buys full insurance at an actuarially fair premium.
preliminaries: A consumer has initial wealth \(W\), faces a loss \(L\in(0,W)\) with probability \(\pi\in(0,1)\), and has expected utility \(E[u(c)]\), where \(u\) is strictly increasing and strictly concave. The consumer can buy \(q\in[0,L]\) units of insurance. One unit pays one unit in the loss state and costs premium \(\rho\) in both states.
question: Assume the premium is actuarially fair: \(\rho=\pi\). Prove that the consumer chooses full insurance \(q=L\).
solution:
Consumption without a loss is
\[
c_N=W-\rho q,
\]
and consumption with a loss is
\[
c_L=W-L-\rho q+q=W-L+(1-\rho)q.
\]
With \(\rho=\pi\), expected consumption is
\[
(1-\pi)c_N+\pi c_L
=(1-\pi)(W-\pi q)+\pi(W-L+(1-\pi)q)
=W-\pi L,
\]
which is independent of \(q\). Thus insurance only changes the riskiness of consumption, not its mean.

Full insurance \(q=L\) equalizes consumption:
\[
c_N=W-\pi L,\qquad c_L=W-L+(1-\pi)L=W-\pi L.
\]
For any \(q<L\), consumption differs across states while having the same mean. Strict concavity implies that the constant consumption allocation with this mean strictly maximizes expected utility among all random consumptions with that mean. Therefore the unique optimum is \(q=L\).

source.locator: choice under uncertainty and risk aversion

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Strict Dominance and Rationalizability
learning_objective: Show that a strictly dominated action cannot be a best response to any belief.
preliminaries:
In a finite normal-form game, action \(a_i\) is strictly dominated by mixed strategy \(\sigma_i\) if for every opponent action profile \(a_{-i}\),
\[
u_i(\sigma_i,a_{-i})>u_i(a_i,a_{-i}).
\]
An action is rationalizable only if it is a best response to some belief over opponents' rationalizable actions.
question: Prove that if \(a_i\) is strictly dominated by a mixed strategy, then \(a_i\) is not rationalizable.
solution:
Let \(\mu_i\) be any belief over opponents' action profiles. Since \(a_i\) is strictly dominated by \(\sigma_i\), we have
\[
u_i(\sigma_i,a_{-i})>u_i(a_i,a_{-i})
\]
for every \(a_{-i}\). Multiplying by \(\mu_i(a_{-i})\) and summing over \(a_{-i}\) gives
\[
\sum_{a_{-i}}\mu_i(a_{-i})u_i(\sigma_i,a_{-i})
>
\sum_{a_{-i}}\mu_i(a_{-i})u_i(a_i,a_{-i}).
\]
Thus \(\sigma_i\) yields strictly higher expected payoff than \(a_i\) under every belief \(\mu_i\). Therefore \(a_i\) is never a best response to any belief. Since rationalizable actions must be justifiable as best responses to some admissible belief, \(a_i\) is not rationalizable.

source.notes: Rewritten as a standalone proof around the standard dominance result.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Mixed Nash Equilibrium in Matching Pennies
learning_objective: Compute a mixed equilibrium by making each player indifferent over pure actions used with positive probability.
preliminaries: In a mixed Nash equilibrium, every pure action used with positive probability gives the same expected payoff, and no unused pure action gives a higher payoff.
question: Two players simultaneously choose \(H\) or \(T\). Player 1 receives \(1\) if the actions match and \(-1\) otherwise. Player 2 receives the negative of player 1's payoff. Find all Nash equilibria.
solution:
There is no pure Nash equilibrium. If the actions match, player 2 wants to switch; if they do not match, player 1 wants to switch.

Let player 1 choose \(H\) with probability \(p\), and player 2 choose \(H\) with probability \(q\). Player 1's expected payoff from \(H\) is \(q(1)+(1-q)(-1)=2q-1\). From \(T\) it is \(q(-1)+(1-q)(1)=1-2q\). If player 1 mixes, these must be equal, so \(2q-1=1-2q\), hence \(q=1/2\).

Player 2 wants to minimize player 1's payoff. Player 2's payoff from \(H\) is the negative of player 1's payoff when player 2 plays \(H\): \(-[p(1)+(1-p)(-1)]=1-2p\). From \(T\) it is \(-[p(-1)+(1-p)(1)]=2p-1\). Indifference gives \(1-2p=2p-1\), so \(p=1/2\).

Therefore the unique Nash equilibrium is for each player to mix equally: \((p,q)=(1/2,1/2)\).

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Mixed Equilibrium in Battle of the Sexes
learning_objective: Find pure and mixed equilibria in a coordination game with conflicting preferences.
preliminaries: In a mixed Nash equilibrium, each player must be indifferent among all pure actions assigned positive probability.
question: Players choose \(B\) or \(S\). Payoffs are \((2,1)\) at \((B,B)\), \((1,2)\) at \((S,S)\), and \((0,0)\) after mismatches. Find all Nash equilibria.
solution:
The pure Nash equilibria are \((B,B)\) and \((S,S)\): at either coordinated outcome, a unilateral deviation gives \(0\), so neither player wants to deviate. Mismatched outcomes are not equilibria because either player can switch to coordinate and get a positive payoff.

For a mixed equilibrium, let player 1 choose \(B\) with probability \(p\), and player 2 choose \(B\) with probability \(q\). Player 1's payoff from \(B\) is \(2q\), and from \(S\) is \(1-q\). Indifference requires \(2q=1-q\), so \(q=1/3\).

Player 2's payoff from \(B\) is \(p\), and from \(S\) is \(2(1-p)\). Indifference requires \(p=2(1-p)\), so \(p=2/3\).

Thus there are three Nash equilibria: \((B,B)\), \((S,S)\), and the mixed equilibrium where player 1 plays \(B\) with probability \(2/3\) and player 2 plays \(B\) with probability \(1/3\).

source.notes: Variant around mixed equilibrium and coordination.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Subgame Perfection in Entry Deterrence
learning_objective: Use backward induction to rule out noncredible threats.
preliminaries: An incumbent and entrant play the following perfect-information game. First the entrant chooses \(In\) or \(Out\). If \(Out\), payoffs are \((0,2)\), where the entrant's payoff is listed first. If \(In\), the incumbent chooses \(Fight\) or \(Accommodate\). \(Fight\) gives \((-1,-1)\), and \(Accommodate\) gives \((1,1)\). A subgame perfect equilibrium must induce a Nash equilibrium in every subgame.
question: Find the subgame perfect equilibrium. Also identify a Nash equilibrium that relies on a noncredible threat.
solution:
Consider the incumbent's decision after entry. If the incumbent fights, its payoff is \(-1\); if it accommodates, its payoff is \(1\). Therefore the incumbent optimally chooses \(Accommodate\).

Anticipating accommodation, the entrant compares \(In\), which gives payoff \(1\), with \(Out\), which gives payoff \(0\). The entrant chooses \(In\). Thus the unique subgame perfect equilibrium is \((In, Accommodate)\).

There is also a Nash equilibrium in strategies \((Out, Fight)\). Given \(Fight\), the entrant prefers \(Out\) because \(0>-1\). Given \(Out\), the incumbent's action is off path, so \(Fight\) is not tested by Nash equilibrium. But the threat to fight is not credible because in the proper subgame after \(In\), the incumbent strictly prefers \(Accommodate\). Therefore \((Out,Fight)\) is Nash but not subgame perfect.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Grim Trigger in an Infinitely Repeated Prisoner's Dilemma
learning_objective: Derive the discount-factor threshold that supports cooperation using grim-trigger strategies.
preliminaries: In each period, two players choose \(C\) or \(D\). Payoffs are \(3,3\) after \((C,C)\), \(0,5\) after \((C,D)\), \(5,0\) after \((D,C)\), and \(1,1\) after \((D,D)\). The game is repeated infinitely with common discount factor \(\delta\in(0,1)\). Grim trigger plays \(C\) until someone defects, and then plays \(D\) forever.
question: For which values of \(\delta\) is the grim-trigger strategy profile a subgame perfect equilibrium?
solution:
After any history with a past defection, grim trigger prescribes \(D\) forever. Since \(D\) is a stage-game best response to \(D\), no player wants to deviate in a punishment phase.

At a cooperative history, following grim trigger gives
\[
\frac{3}{1-\delta}.
\]
A one-period deviation gives \(5\) today and then triggers permanent punishment, yielding
\[
5+\frac{\delta}{1-\delta}.
\]
Cooperation is optimal if
\[
\frac{3}{1-\delta}\ge 5+\frac{\delta}{1-\delta}.
\]
Multiplying by \(1-\delta>0\) gives \(3\ge 5-4\delta\), so \(\delta\ge 1/2\).

By the one-shot deviation principle for discounted repeated games with finite action sets, grim trigger is a subgame perfect equilibrium exactly when \(\delta\ge 1/2\).

source.locator: repeated games and folk theorem exercises
source.notes: Complete threshold computation for a classic repeated-game family.

validation.notes: Seed record written to the curated schema; awaiting independent validation.

title: Nash Bargaining on a Linear Frontier
learning_objective: Compute the Nash bargaining solution by maximizing the Nash product over feasible utility gains.
preliminaries: In a two-player Nash bargaining problem with disagreement point \(d=(d_1,d_2)\), the Nash solution maximizes \((u_1-d_1)(u_2-d_2)\) over feasible utility pairs \(u\) that weakly dominate \(d\).
question:
Let the feasible utility set be
\[
U=\{(u_1,u_2)\in\mathbb{R}^2_+:u_1+u_2\le 10\}
\]
and let the disagreement point be \(d=(2,2)\). Compute the Nash bargaining solution.
solution:
Only points with \(u_1\ge 2\) and \(u_2\ge 2\) are individually rational. Since the Nash product is increasing in each utility above disagreement, the solution lies on the Pareto frontier \(u_1+u_2=10\). Write \(u_2=10-u_1\), with \(u_1\in[2,8]\). The Nash product is
\[
N(u_1)=(u_1-2)(u_2-2)=(u_1-2)(8-u_1).
\]
This is a concave quadratic. Its derivative is
\[
N'(u_1)=8-u_1-(u_1-2)=10-2u_1.
\]
Setting \(N'(u_1)=0\) gives \(u_1=5\), and then \(u_2=5\). Therefore the Nash bargaining solution is \((5,5)\).

source.notes: Numerical bargaining computation inspired by the Nash solution chapter.
